beats.prompt: Before we count arrangements, refresh two tools: factorial and exact fractions.

interaction.title: n! and fractions — a quick refresh
interaction.body: **n! (n factorial)** = n·(n−1)·…·1. Quick table: 3!=6, 4!=24, 5!=120. An **exact fraction** like 5/54 means 5 favourable outcomes out of 54 total — no decimals, no rounding. These two tools power everything in this lesson.

feedback.correct: Two tools ready — n! and exact fractions.
hints: Read the card, then tap Continue.
A fraction like 5/54 is exact: 5 favourable out of 54 total.

beats.prompt: Match each factorial to its value — these shrinking products power everything ahead.

feedback.correct: 3!=6, 4!=24, 5!=120 — the shrinking products that power nPk.
hints: Multiply down from n: 3·2·1=?

beats.prompt: From 5 candidates pick 3. Case A: they become President, VP, and Treasurer. Case B: they're just '3 people on a committee.' Same number of ways — or different?

options: Different — the roles version is bigger

Same number of ways.note: The classic trap — roles make order matter, so ranked slots count k!=6× more than a committee.

Different — the roles version is bigger.note: Right — ranked roles distinguish orderings: 60 ordered vs 10 unordered, ×3! apart.

Different — the committee is bigger.note: They differ, but adding order can only raise the count: 60 ordered > 10 unordered.

beats.prompt: The 5 candidates are Anya, Ben, Cara, Dan, and Eva. Tap 3 to fill the President, VP, and Treasurer slots — then tap Submit to count how many ways.
beats.interviewNote: The 52! possible orderings of a full 52-card deck (Knuth/Fisher–Yates shuffle, GB p.89) are nP52 = 52! — nPk with k=n. Every permutation equally likely. That's the natural scale-up from 5·4·3 = 60.

feedback.correct: Yes — 5·4·3=60; ranked slots mean order matters, each pick shrinks the pool.
hints: How many choices for slot 1? For slot 2? For slot 3?
The pool shrinks with each pick: 5 choices, then 4, then 3 — multiply them.

beats.prompt: Read through the worked example — nPk(5,3) step by step — then continue.

interaction.title: nPk(5,3) = 5·4·3 — the shrinking-product structure
interaction.body: Slot 1: 5 choices. Slot 2: 4 remain (the chosen person can't reappear). Slot 3: 3 remain. Product: 5·4·3 = 60. General: nPk = n·(n−1)·…·(n−k+1) = n!/(n−k)!.

hints: Slot 1: 5 choices; slot 2: 4 remain; slot 3: 3 remain.
Continue.

beats.prompt: Select any 3 people, then flip the Order toggle ON↔OFF. Watch the count jump between 60 and 10 — why are they exactly ×3! apart?

feedback.correct: Order ON=60(nPk), OFF=10(nCk). Gap=×3!=6 — each set of 3 has exactly 6 ordered versions.
hints: Set order ON first and count; then flip the toggle OFF.
Order OFF groups k! re-orderings of each set into one — count shrinks by k!=3!=6.

beats.interviewNote: C(52,5) = 2,598,960 total five-card hands (GB p.34). Four-of-a-kind = 13 × 48 = 624 (13 ranks × 48 remaining cards). Any combinatorics interview question about card games bottoms out at C(52,5) and these counts.
beats.prompt: Tap each lens card to reveal the formula — then watch all three converge on C(52,5) = 2,598,960.

lenses.label: nCk = nPk/k!
lenses.body: nCk = nPk/k! = n!/(k!(n−k)!). Each set of 5 cards has 5!=120 orderings → 311,875,200 ÷ 120 = 2,598,960.

lenses.label: ×k! link
lenses.body: nPk = nCk × k!. So 2,598,960 unordered hands × 120 orderings = 311,875,200 ordered arrangements.

feedback.correct: nPk ordered, nCk=nPk/k! unordered. C(52,5)=2,598,960 five-card hands — the formula scales.
hints: Reveal the nPk card first.
nCk = nPk/k! — divides out the k! orderings of each set.
×k! link: nPk = nCk × k!.

beats.prompt: Three dice each show a different value. Step 1: what fraction of all orderings of those 3 values is strictly increasing? Step 2: what is the probability all three dice come up in strictly increasing order?

feedback.correct: Right — only 1 of 3!=6 orderings is increasing → 1/6; full P = C(6,3)/6³ = 20/216 = 5/54.
hints: Three distinct values have 3!=6 equally likely orderings; exactly 1 is strictly increasing → 1/3!=1/6.
P(one specific order) = 1/6. Full prob: C(6,3)=20 triples of distinct values from 6; total outcomes 6³=216.

beats.prompt: Deal a standard 52-card deck to 4 players (13 cards each). The 4 aces land in 4 distinct piles. How many ways can the aces be distributed — one per pile?

interaction.scenario: The aces look identical in rank — but the 4 piles are distinct destinations. Does order matter here?

feedback.correct: Exactly — 4!=24; the piles are distinct destinations, so the aces' order matters.
hints: Each ace lands in a different pile — are the 4 piles distinguishable?
The piles are distinct (each player's hand) → 4 distinct slots → 4! = 4·3·2·1.

beats.prompt: Tap through the recap cards — and catch the bridge to Lesson 3.

feedback.correct: Order matters → nPk; order doesn't → nCk=nPk/k!. These nCk are the binomial coefficients — L3 builds Pascal's triangle from them.
hints: Order matters → nPk = n!/(n−k)!.
Order doesn't → nCk = nPk/k! = n!/(k!(n−k)!).
L3 preview: every nCk is a binomial coefficient.
